result:
`vfs_read` is a normal (non-static) function, confirmed. Here's the complete answer.

## `ksys_read()` — fs/read_write.c:602-619

Direct calls, in source order:

| Line | Call | Kind |
|---|---|---|
| 604 | `fdget_pos(fd)` | `static inline` function, `include/linux/file.h:72`. Not a macro — it's `return __to_fd(__fdget_pos(fd));` internally, but that's a nested call, not part of ksys_read's own call list. |
| 608 | `file_ppos(f.file)` | `static inline` function defined just above in the same file, `fs/read_write.c:597-600` (`return file->f_mode & FMODE_STREAM ? NULL : &file->f_pos;`). |
| 613 | `vfs_read(f.file, buf, count, ppos)` | Regular (non-static, non-inline) function, defined `fs/read_write.c:450`. |
| 616 | `fdput_pos(f)` | `static inline` function, `include/linux/file.h:77`. |

No macros are involved — every apparent call site resolves to a real function (two of them `static inline`, which look macro-like but aren't). Verified both by direct source reading and by cross-checking against cbm's graph: `trace_path` on `ksys_read` outbound returned nothing, because in this project's index `CALLS` edges are attached to the file/Module node (`fs/read_write.c`) rather than to individual `Function` nodes — querying `(Module fs/read_write.c)-[:CALLS]->(Function)` returns all 69 functions called anywhere in the file (a superset), which does include `fdget_pos`, `file_ppos`, `vfs_read`, and `fdput_pos`, consistent with the manual read but not scoped precisely to `ksys_read` alone — hence source inspection was the authoritative source for the exact 4 call sites above.
